sections.heading: Why Data Matters
sections.body: Machine learning systems aren't given explicit rules — they infer patterns from example data. The more representative that data is of the situations the system will actually face, the better it tends to generalize once it's in real use.

sections.heading: Training Data vs. Real-World Data
sections.body: A model only learns what's present in its training data. If the training examples don't represent the full range of situations it'll encounter later, the model can fail in ways that seem surprising from the outside — this is where problems like bias and overfitting come from.

sections.heading: What "Learning" Means Here
sections.body: In this context, "learning" means adjusting a model's internal numbers (its parameters) so its outputs get closer to correct answers across many training examples. It's a mathematical optimization process, not learning the way a person studies a subject.

quiz.question: Why can a model fail on real-world situations it wasn't trained on?
options: Its patterns were learned only from its training data, which may not represent every case

quiz.question: In machine learning, "learning" primarily refers to:
options: Adjusting internal parameters to reduce error over training examples

quiz.question: More representative training data generally leads to:
